For each susceptible occupation, we first identify skill-similar alternatives across all Canadian occupations. This figure traces the filtering that follows for Material handlers (TEER 5) in Oxford, ON.
Skills-Similar
& TEER-Compatible
Local Presence
Outlook
& Earnings
Community Review
Starting from all skill-similar occupations, we screen for comparable training requirements (TEER ±1) and select the top ~30 most similar candidates. Across these panels, the ■ square is the susceptible occupation, each ● dot is a candidate — closer to centre = more similar — and dots that fade have been filtered out. Different use cases may apply stricter or looser thresholds.
We screen candidates for existing local relevance. Dark blue = present in the Census Division, lighter blue = present in the province but not locally. Due to data limitations, provincial-level presence is used here; finer geographic filtering could strengthen results where local data exists.
Surviving candidates are screened for employment outlook (COPS projections) and earnings viability (≥65% of the susceptible occupation's wage). Orange = passes both, yellow = fails outlook, red = fails earnings. An additional AI-exposure filter is applied but does not exclude any candidates here.
Remaining candidates are presented for community validation, which may surface additional occupations or relax earlier filters based on local plans and goals. We exclude occupations that are themselves susceptible and ◆ highlight those potentially relevant based on extrapolations from surrounding-area and community profiles. Colour denotes TEER level: dark green = TEER 5, mid green = TEER 4.
Note: Data limitations — particularly the lack of granular local labour market information — constrain the precision of each filter and the conclusions that can be drawn. Despite this, the filtering framework itself demonstrates the potential of systematic transition planning — and a strong rationale for public investment in better local labour market data.
